summary: This 2005 blog post is a guest review by professional cabinetmaker Mike Ewanchuk of Finger Gloves, a product designed for hand-staining wood. The review compares Finger Gloves against standard disposable latex gloves during a real staining project. Mike finds that Finger Gloves dramatically outperform latex in durability, grip, and reusability, requiring zero changes during a full coat versus six pairs of latex gloves. The post is part of Dan Shapiro's 'free tool review' series on his woodworking blog Nothing Severed Yet.
key_points: Finger Gloves lasted an entire staining session without tearing, compared to six pairs of disposable latex gloves for the same project
The product offers better grip and dexterity than latex gloves thanks to textured nubs on the fingertips
Despite higher per-unit cost, the reusability and durability make Finger Gloves more economical than disposable latex
The gloves have crossover utility beyond staining, such as rose trimming for thorn protection
money_quotes: I firmly maintain that these facts are unrelated, because admitting otherwise would mean having to clean my shop.
By the time the box was done, I had gone through six pairs of latex gloves.
I was able to cover the entire box without changing gloves once.
You will almost forget you are wearing them.
His shop is so clean you could eat off the floor (and his one year old daughter occasionally does).
key_insight: For hand-staining work, reusable finger-specific gloves can be more durable, practical, and cost-effective than disposable latex gloves.
